title: Matching: A New Proof for an Ancient Algorithm
speaker: Vijay Vazirani
abstract: For all practical purposes, the Micali-Vazirani algorithm, discovered in 1980, is still the most efficient known maximum matching algorithm (for very dense graphs, slight asymptotic improvement can be obtained using fast matrix multiplication). However, this has remained a ``black box" result for the last 32 years. We hope to change this with the help of a recent paper giving a simpler proof and exposition of the algorithm: http://arxiv.org/abs/1210.4594

proof_stages.label: Augmenting-path phase framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk reduces maximum matching to repeated augmentation and then to phases that find a maximal set of vertex-disjoint shortest augmenting paths, aiming for O(m) work per phase and O(sqrt n) phases.

proof_stages.label: Bipartite BFS predecessor template
proof_stages.summary: In bipartite graphs, alternating BFS from all unmatched vertices on one side assigns levels and predecessors, after which backtracking and deleting used vertices yields a maximal set of disjoint shortest augmenting paths in linear time.

proof_stages.label: Parity obstruction in general graphs
proof_stages.summary: For non-bipartite graphs, vertices may have distinct even and odd levels, minimum alternating paths are not BFS-honest, and finding the needed parity paths appears to require a new structural theory rather than straightforward search.

proof_stages.label: Bridges, buds, petals, and double DFS
proof_stages.summary: The general-graph search distinguishes props from bridges, uses bridges to assign max levels up to bottlenecks, and introduces double depth-first search with petal/blossom-node marking to find bases or augmentations while skipping nested structures efficiently.

proof_stages.label: Tenacity and recursive blossoms
proof_stages.summary: Tenacity, defined from even and odd levels, restores enough BFS-honesty to prove uniqueness of the higher-tenacity base on all min/max paths, yielding iterated bases and a recursive graph-theoretic definition of blossoms.

proof_stages.label: Supports and synchronized event schedule
proof_stages.summary: The algorithm assigns edge tenacities, runs each search level as a min-level BFS step followed exactly by double DFS on bridges of tenacity 2i+1, and relies on the main theorem that every vertex of a given tenacity lies in the support of a unique appropriate bridge on a max-level path.

proof_stages.label: Path reconstruction, deletion, and linear-time implementation
proof_stages.summary: After reaching unmatched vertices, blossom nodes store enough bridge-endpoint and base information to patch an augmenting path, remove the whole affected blossom safely using iterated-base properties, and achieve linear-time phases with suitable skipping/data-structure support.
